title: Crime
subtitle: Crime, noir, and detective films — cerebral over violent. No gore for gore's sake.

sections.name: I. High Priority — Best Entry Points
sections.desc: Given your love of structurally rigorous thrillers (All the President's Men, Hunt for Red October), these will land.

items.title: Chinatown
items.whyPriority: The neo-noir foundation. Robert Towne's screenplay is widely considered the greatest ever written. Nicholson at peak form, Faye Dunaway, John Huston as one of cinema's great villains. Procedural intelligence + devastating ending. Direct descendant of All the President's Men's investigative DNA.
items.pitch: Private detective Jake Gittes investigates an adultery case in 1937 Los Angeles. The case becomes something much larger involving water, land, and family.

critics.quote: Won Best Original Screenplay. Eleven nominations including Best Picture.
Routinely cited among the greatest American films. AFI Top 25.

items.title: L.A. Confidential
items.whyPriority: James Ellroy adaptation. Three LAPD detectives in 1950s Los Angeles. Crowe and Pearce career-launching, Spacey at peak charm, Kim Basinger Oscar-winning. Smart structure, ensemble character work, full LA-as-character craftsmanship.
items.pitch: Three very different cops investigate a coffee-shop massacre in 1953 LA. The investigation reveals corruption at every level.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars (Adapted Screenplay, Supporting Actress). Nine total nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: Zodiac
items.whyPriority: Fincher's procedural masterpiece. The Zodiac killer investigation across decades. All the President's Men is its DNA — patient, evidence-driven, character-grounded. Gyllenhaal, Downey Jr., Ruffalo. Slow-burn and richly rewarding.
items.pitch: A San Francisco newspaper cartoonist, a reporter, and an SFPD detective spend decades chasing the Zodiac killer. The case is never officially solved.

critics.quote: Widely considered Fincher's masterpiece. Multiple critics' top-10 lists for the 2000s.

items.title: Memories of Murder
items.whyPriority: Bong's pre-Parasite masterpiece. Two rural detectives investigate Korea's first serial killer. Procedural that becomes character study that becomes elegy. The case was unsolved for 30 years; Bong made the film before the killer was caught.
items.pitch: 1986 South Korea. Two detectives investigate a rural serial killing. Tonal mastery between procedural, dark comedy, and quiet horror.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as one of the greatest Korean films. Bong's career-launching work.

items.title: Prisoners
items.whyPriority: Villeneuve before Sicario/Arrival/Dune. Two girls go missing on Thanksgiving; the desperate father and the meticulous detective converge from opposite ends. Hugh Jackman + Jake Gyllenhaal at career peaks. Roger Deakins cinematography.
items.pitch: Two children disappear. Their father is convinced the police suspect knows where they are. The detective on the case is the most patient character in modern crime cinema.

critics.quote: Roger Deakins Oscar nomination. Many critics named it the best film of 2013.

sections.name: II. Medium Priority — Strong Probable Fits
sections.desc: Likely to land. The detective character pieces and procedurals.

items.title: The Long Goodbye
items.whyPriority: Altman's Marlowe — anachronistic, mumbling, cat-loving. Elliott Gould as Chandler's detective dropped into 1970s LA. Polarizing then, beloved now. The most idiosyncratic detective film of its era.
items.pitch: Philip Marlowe drives a friend to Tijuana. The friend's wife is found dead. Marlowe is a suspect. Improvisation and cigarettes follow.

critics.quote: Initially divisive; reputation has only grown. Routinely cited among Altman's best.

items.title: The Maltese Falcon
items.whyPriority: The classic noir foundation. Bogart as Sam Spade. Sydney Greenstreet's debut. Mary Astor. Every detective film since owes it a debt. Tight, dialogue-driven, definitive.
items.pitch: Private detective Sam Spade is hired by a woman to follow her sister. The sister isn't real. Several murders later, a black bird becomes important.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as the foundational American noir. Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Double Indemnity
items.whyPriority: Wilder + Chandler screenplay. Insurance salesman seduced into murdering a woman's husband. The dialogue is Chandler at his sharpest. The structure is Wilder's gift. Co-listed in pre-1960 classics.
items.pitch: Insurance investigator Walter Neff tells the story of how a woman convinced him to kill her husband for the policy.

critics.quote: Seven Oscar nominations. Routinely listed among the greatest noir films.

items.title: Heat
items.whyPriority: Listed on heist tracker too. Mann's procedural about parallel professionals — De Niro's career thief and Pacino's career detective. The downtown LA shootout reset action cinema.
items.pitch: Cross-listed with the heist tracker. Three hours of professional crime and professional detection.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest crime films ever made.

items.title: The Departed
items.whyPriority: Scorsese's Boston remake of Infernal Affairs. Won Best Picture (his first). DiCaprio, Damon, Nicholson, Wahlberg. The mole-and-rat double-narrative is structurally satisfying.
items.pitch: An undercover cop infiltrates a Boston mob. A mob plant rises through Boston PD. The two converge.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Picture, Best Director (Scorsese's first), Best Adapted Screenplay.

items.title: Drive
items.whyPriority: Listed on heist tracker too. Refn's neo-noir. Gosling's stunt driver. Synth score, neon, slow then sudden violence.
items.pitch: Cross-listed with heist tracker.

critics.quote: Best Director.

items.title: The Secret in Their Eyes
items.whyPriority: Argentine. A retired investigator returns to a 25-year-old murder case. Won Best Foreign Language Film. Slow-burn, structural, devastating.
items.pitch: A retired federal investigator returns to write about a 1974 murder case. The investigation continues alongside a love story he never resolved.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Mulholland Drive
items.pitch: Lynch's masterpiece — also lives on the auteur tracker. Detective story dissolved into dream-logic about Hollywood. Regularly tops 21st-century best-of polls.

critics.quote: Voted #8 in the Greatest Films of All Time poll.

items.title: Touch of Evil
items.pitch: Welles directing himself as a corrupt Mexican border-town detective. The opening tracking shot is one of the most studied in cinema. Cross-listed with Welles auteur and pre-1960.

critics.quote: Considered one of the greatest noir films and one of Welles's best.

items.title: Se7en
items.pitch: Fincher's serial-killer breakthrough. Pitt and Freeman. The ending is famous; the procedural intelligence around it is what makes it last.

critics.quote: Major commercial and critical hit. Established Fincher's career.

items.title: Mystic River
items.pitch: Eastwood adapting Dennis Lehane. Three childhood friends in Boston after one of their daughters is murdered. Penn won Best Actor.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars (Penn Best Actor, Robbins Best Supporting). Six total nominations.

items.title: Gone Baby Gone
items.pitch: Affleck's directorial debut, also Lehane. Boston PI investigates a missing child. Casey Affleck's career-launching role.

critics.quote: Critical breakthrough for Affleck-as-director. Amy Ryan Oscar nomination.

items.title: Insomnia (2002)
items.pitch: Nolan's pre-Batman remake of a Norwegian thriller. Pacino + Robin Williams (cast against type). Alaska, sleep deprivation, moral compromise.

critics.quote: Underrated in the Nolan filmography. Better-regarded as time passes.

items.title: Insomnia (1997)
items.pitch: The Norwegian original. Stellan Skarsgård. Generally considered superior to Nolan's remake.

critics.quote: Critically beloved on release; established Skjoldbjærg as a major Nordic filmmaker.

items.title: Wind River
items.pitch: Sheridan directing himself. Wyoming Indian reservation, FBI, a young woman dead. Jeremy Renner career-best.

critics.quote: Strong critical reception. Sheridan's directorial breakthrough.

items.title: Hell or High Water
items.pitch: West Texas brothers rob banks; Bridges's ranger pursues. Modern Western-crime hybrid. Sheridan screenplay.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

sections.name: IV. Detective Character Pieces
sections.desc: Where the investigation matters less than who's investigating.

items.title: The Big Lebowski
items.pitch: Cross-listed from comedy where you've already loved it. Belongs here too — it's a detective story (kidnapping, ransom, severed toe) wrapped in stoner comedy.

items.title: Inherent Vice
items.pitch: PTA adapting Pynchon. 1970 Los Angeles, hippie PI Doc Sportello, a missing real-estate mogul. Closest spiritual descendant of The Long Goodbye.

critics.quote: Polarizing. Either you sync with the rhythm or you don't.

items.title: The Nice Guys
items.pitch: Crowe and Gosling as 1977 LA private detectives. Buddy comedy + neo-noir. Underseen.

critics.quote: Critical hit, commercial underperformer. Strong cult following.

items.title: Brick
items.pitch: Johnson's debut. High-school noir. Hard-boiled detective dialogue from teenage characters. Concept could fail; execution is superb.

critics.quote: Originality of Vision Award. Cult standing.

items.title: Oldboy
items.pitch: Korean revenge masterpiece. Vengeance trilogy middle film. Stylized, brutal, structurally clever. The hammer hallway sequence is famous. Some violence intensity — but never gratuitous.

critics.quote: Won the Grand Prix.

items.title: The Vanishing (1988)
items.pitch: Cross-listed from horror. Crime/thriller at its most patient and devastating.

items.title: I Saw the Devil
items.pitch: Korean revenge film. Secret agent hunts the serial killer who murdered his fiancée. Considerably more violent than most on this list — flagged accordingly.

critics.quote: Polarizing for violence. Critically beloved by genre critics; mainstream audiences split.

items.title: A Prophet
items.pitch: Cross-listed from heist. Six years in a French prison. Slow-build crime epic.

sections.name: VI. Auteur Filmographies
sections.desc: Primary genre home for director-specific crime works appearing in the Auteur tab.

items.title: Sicario
items.whyPriority: Cartel thriller that's smarter than the genre. Blunt, Del Toro, Brolin. Deakins again. Taylor Sheridan screenplay.
items.pitch: An idealistic FBI agent is recruited into a black-ops task force on the Mexican border.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Blue Velvet
items.whyPriority: His breakthrough. American suburb's underbelly. Hopper at his most terrifying.
items.pitch: A young man finds a severed ear in a field. The investigation reveals what's underneath his small town.

critics.quote: Best Director nomination.

items.title: Lost Highway
items.pitch: A jazz musician is accused of murdering his wife. Then becomes someone else. Lynch at his most fragmented.

items.title: Miller's Crossing
items.whyPriority: Their best gangster film. Prohibition-era Irish/Italian war. Gabriel Byrne's career-best.
items.pitch: An advisor to an Irish mob boss navigates a war with the Italians. Loyalty, betrayal, ethics.

critics.quote: Initially modest performance; critical reputation has grown enormously.

items.title: Blood Simple
items.pitch: Their debut. Texas noir. A husband, his wife's affair, a hired killer who has his own ideas.

critics.quote: Sundance Grand Jury Prize. Established their career immediately.

sections.name: Z. Plex History (Auto-Promoted)
sections.desc: Items auto-promoted from Plex viewing history and TMDB recommendations during 2026-05-08 sync. Source attribution is in each item's pitch.
